sub_tasks.requirements: Method and code fidelity to the paper

The All-CNN-C model is implemented by replacing every max-pooling layer with a standard convolutional layer of stride r=2 and 3×3 kernel whose number of output channels equals the number of input channels, yielding a network of only convolutional layers plus a final global-average + softmax, as described in Section 2 and Table 2.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: The Strided-CNN-C variant removes each max-pooling layer and increases the stride of the immediately preceding convolution by 1 (to stride 2) so the next layer covers the same spatial region, as described in Section 3.1 and Table 2.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: The ConvPool-CNN-C control variant inserts a dense convolution with the same kernel size as the respective pooling layer immediately before each max-pooling layer, used to control for parameter count, as described in Section 3.1 and Table 2.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: Base models A, B, C are implemented per Table 1: model B is the Network-in-Network variant with one 1×1 convolution after each normal convolution, model C replaces all 5×5 convolutions with stacked 3×3 convolutions, and all three terminate in a 1×1 convolution producing 10 outputs followed by global average pooling over all positions and softmax (Section 3.1).
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: Guided backpropagation is implemented so that, when propagating a signal back through a ReLU, an entry is zeroed if at least one of the top gradient OR the bottom (forward) activation is negative — combining the deconvnet and backprop masks — as defined in Section 4 and Figure 1c.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: Data and preprocessing fidelity
CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 input images are whitened and contrast normalized following Goodfellow et al. (2013), as stated in Section 3.2.
The data-augmentation pipeline adds horizontally flipped copies of all images plus randomly translated versions with a maximum translation of 5 pixels in each dimension, matching Section 3.2.
Experiments load the three datasets named in the paper — CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100 (Krizhevsky & Hinton 2009) and the ILSVRC-2012 ImageNet subset (Deng et al. 2009) — as stated in Section 3.
Experiment execution and reproducibility

CIFAR training uses stochastic gradient descent with fixed momentum 0.9, a total of 350 epochs, learning-rate schedule S=[200,250,300] multiplying γ by 0.1 at those epochs, and selects the best γ from the fixed set {0.25, 0.1, 0.05, 0.01}, per Section 3.2.
Dropout is applied at 20% to the input image and 50% after each pooling layer (or its replacement layer), and all models use weight decay λ=0.001, exactly as specified in Section 3.2.

The ImageNet All-CNN-B run uses 12 convolutional layers (conv1–conv12), 450,000 iterations with batches of 64, initial learning rate γ=0.01 divided by 10 every 200,000 iterations, and weight decay λ=0.0005, as described in Section 3.3.
All experiments are run with the Caffe framework (Jia et al. 2014) as stated in Section 3.
Evaluation protocol and metric correctness
CIFAR results report test classification error using only the best γ for each model from the fixed search set, as specified in Section 3.2.

The pooling-necessity claim is evaluated by a parameter-matched comparison of All-CNN versus ConvPool-CNN (rather than versus the smaller base model) to rule out that gains stem only from added parameters, per Section 3.1 and Section 3.2.
ImageNet performance is reported as Top-1 validation error evaluated only on the center 224×224 patch, as stated in Section 3.3.
The CIFAR-10 ablation evaluates the full grid of base models A/B/C crossed with their Base, Strided-CNN, All-CNN and ConvPool-CNN variants without augmentation, as reported in Table 3 (Section 3.2).
Result match versus the paper's reported targets
The simplest model (base model A) reproduces approximately 12.5% test error on CIFAR-10 without augmentation, as reported in Section 3.2 and Table 3.
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: All-CNN-C matches or beats the best previously reported CIFAR-10 result both with and without data augmentation, reproducing the Table 4 comparison (Section 3.2).
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: The qualitative ablation outcome is reproduced: Strided-CNN underperforms its base model while All-CNN performs on par with ConvPool-CNN, supporting that max-pooling is not strictly necessary (Section 3.2, Table 3).
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: The upscaled All-CNN-B reaches a Top-1 validation error of ~41.2% on ILSVRC-2012 (comparable to the 40.7% of Krizhevsky et al. 2012) with fewer than 10 million parameters, as reported in Section 3.3.
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: On CIFAR-100 the All-CNN-C performs comparably to the state of the art and is outperformed only by the much larger (~50M parameter) fractional max-pooling network of Graham (2015), per Section 3.2 and Table 4.
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: Artifact completeness and provenance

Layer-by-layer architecture specifications for the base/derived CIFAR networks (Tables 1–2) and the 12-layer ImageNet network (Table 6) are provided as code or config matching the paper (Section 3.1 and Appendix).
Feature-visualization artifacts are produced with guided backpropagation for the lower (conv1–conv3) and higher (conv6, conv9) ImageNet layers, reproducing Figures 2 and 3 (Section 4).
